questions.question: Define an abstract class in Java and explain its purpose.
keyPoints: Declared with the abstract keyword
Cannot be instantiated directly
Serves as a base class for subclasses

questions.question: Explain the characteristics of abstract classes in Java with an example.
keyPoints: Abstract classes are declared with the abstract keyword
Can have both abstract methods (no body) and concrete methods (with body)
Cannot be instantiated directly
Must be inherited by other classes
Can have constructors, fields, and concrete methods
Code example demonstrating the characteristics

questions.question: Compare and contrast abstract classes and interfaces in Java.
keyPoints: Both cannot be instantiated directly
Abstract classes can have both abstract and concrete methods
Interfaces can only have abstract methods (Java 7 and earlier) or default and static methods (Java 8 and later)
Abstract classes can have constructors and fields
Interfaces cannot have constructors and fields
Use cases for abstract classes and interfaces

questions.question: Explain the rules governing abstract classes in Java and provide an example.
keyPoints: A class with any abstract method must be declared abstract
Concrete subclasses must implement all abstract methods
Abstract classes can have constructors, fields, and concrete methods
Cannot instantiate an abstract class directly
Code example demonstrating the rules

questions.question: List the benefits of using abstract classes in Java.
keyPoints: Provide a common base with shared implementation
Force subclasses to implement specific methods
Enable code reuse
